name: Lattice Performance Management API
what_it_does: Lattice's API provides programmatic access to performance review cycles, OKR/goal tracking, employee engagement surveys, 1-on-1 meeting notes, and compensation management data for people-ops automation.
use_cases: Trigger performance review cycle creation and send automated review reminders based on employee anniversary dates fetched from an HRIS
Read completed review scores and comments to populate a manager dashboard or feed into a compensation calibration workflow
Sync OKR progress updates from a project management tool into Lattice goal records to keep performance data current
Query engagement survey results and eNPS trends to surface early signals of attrition risk to an HR analytics agent
Create and assign 1-on-1 meeting agendas automatically based on upcoming review cycles or goal check-in cadences
not_for: Payroll processing or compensation disbursement — Lattice manages compensation planning data but does not execute payments
Applicant tracking or recruiting workflows — Lattice focuses on post-hire employee lifecycle, not candidate management
Time tracking or scheduling — workforce management functions are outside Lattice's scope
best_when: Your organization uses Lattice as its performance and engagement platform and you want to automate review cycles, goal syncing, or surface people-analytics data in other tools.
avoid_when: You need a full HRIS or payroll system — Lattice is a performance layer that works alongside an HRIS, not a replacement for one.

auth.notes: API key authentication is available for server-to-server integrations. OAuth 2.0 is supported for user-delegated access. Scopes map to Lattice product areas (performance, goals, surveys, compensation). Keys are generated per integration in the Lattice admin settings panel.

pricing.paid_tiers: API access is included with Lattice platform subscriptions. Lattice pricing is per-employee-per-month, ranging roughly $11-15/employee/month for core performance features; full platform (including compensation and engagement) runs higher. No standalone API tier.
pricing.notes: A developer sandbox is available via the Lattice partner program. Direct customers get API access through their existing subscription admin portal.

performance.rate_limits: Default rate limit is 100 requests per minute per API key. Higher limits available upon request for enterprise customers. GraphQL queries count as single requests regardless of payload size.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: GraphQL errors are well-structured with path and message fields. REST endpoint errors return standard HTTP status codes with JSON bodies. Error messages are generally descriptive but GraphQL schema documentation has gaps for newer compensation-related fields.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read operations are fully idempotent. Write operations like creating review requests lack explicit idempotency keys; duplicate creation calls may result in duplicate records if not caught by the caller.
known_agent_gotchas: Review cycle IDs are not stable across environments — sandbox cycle IDs cannot be used to test production flows without re-fetching current cycle state
The GraphQL schema evolves frequently with new performance and compensation features; agents must handle unknown fields gracefully or pin to a schema version
Employee records in Lattice use Lattice-internal IDs that must be mapped from your HRIS employee IDs — no direct lookup by email or external ID in all endpoints
Engagement survey responses are anonymized at the API layer when group sizes are below a configurable threshold; agents querying survey data may receive null or aggregated responses unexpectedly
Webhook delivery is not guaranteed to be in-order for performance review state transitions; agents must fetch current state after receiving any event rather than inferring state from event sequence

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced on all endpoints. API keys are long-lived and should be treated as secrets; rotation requires manual intervention in the admin portal. OAuth scopes provide reasonable product-area granularity but cannot restrict access to specific review cycles or employee subsets.
